title: Quiz: Barren Plateaus
description: Test your understanding of the barren plateau phenomenon and mitigation strategies

questions.question: What is the defining characteristic of a barren plateau?

options.text: The cost function has many local minima
Gradient variance decreases exponentially with system size
The circuit has too few parameters
The quantum state becomes maximally entangled

questions.explanation: A barren plateau occurs when Var[gradient] <= O(b^{-n}) for b > 1. This means gradients become exponentially small with qubit count n, making optimization impossible as the landscape becomes flat.

questions.question: Which type of cost function is MOST susceptible to barren plateaus?
questions.explanation: Global fidelity costs that depend on the entire quantum state (like |<0|psi>|^2) are most susceptible to barren plateaus. They involve all qubits simultaneously, leading to exponential gradient suppression.

questions.question: How does circuit depth affect barren plateaus?

options.text: Deeper circuits always avoid barren plateaus
Depth has no effect on gradient variance
Deeper circuits are more susceptible to barren plateaus
Only circuits with depth exactly n have barren plateaus

questions.explanation: Deeper circuits approach 2-designs (Haar-random), which guarantees barren plateaus. The gradient variance typically decreases as circuit depth increases beyond a critical point.

questions.question: What is identity initialization and why does it help?

options.text: Setting all parameters to pi, making the circuit complex
Initializing parameters near zero, keeping circuit close to identity
Setting parameters equal to the number of qubits

questions.explanation: Identity initialization sets parameters near zero (with small noise), so the circuit is close to the identity operation. This avoids the exponentially flat regions found in random deep circuits.

questions.question: Layer-by-layer training can help mitigate barren plateaus by keeping the circuit effectively shallow during early training.
questions.explanation: True. By training one layer at a time while freezing previous layers, the effective circuit depth during training stays low. This maintains reasonable gradient magnitudes and avoids the barren plateau of the full deep circuit.

questions.question: For a 10-qubit system with barren plateau, approximately what is the gradient magnitude?
questions.explanation: For barren plateaus, gradient variance scales as O(1/2^n). For n=10 qubits, this is O(1/1024) ~ 0.001. The gradient magnitude is roughly sqrt(variance) ~ 0.03, but concentration means most gradients are much smaller.

questions.question: Which circuit depth scaling is recommended to avoid barren plateaus?

options.text: O(log n) - logarithmic in qubit count

questions.explanation: Logarithmic depth O(log n) is recommended because it provides sufficient expressibility while avoiding the exponential gradient suppression of deep circuits. This is sometimes called the 'Goldilocks zone'.

questions.question: Which strategies can help mitigate barren plateaus? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Making circuits as deep as possible

questions.explanation: Local cost functions, identity initialization, and problem-inspired ansatze all help avoid barren plateaus. Deep circuits actually make the problem worse by approaching Haar-random behavior.
